title: Cracking the Code: A High Schooler's Guide to Choosing AP Classes
author: Tklesson Team
content:
Walking into AP class selection without a plan is like navigating a maze blindfolded. Here's what nobody tells you about choosing the right courses.

Start With Your Strengths

The best AP class to take is the one you're genuinely interested in. AP Psychology fascinates you? Take it. AP Physics makes you want to cry? Skip it. Passion beats perceived prestige every time.

Look at your current grades. If you're struggling in regular Biology, AP Biology will not be easier. Be honest with yourself.

Consider Your Workload

One AP class requires 3-5 hours of homework per week. Three AP classes require 9-15 hours. Add sports, clubs, a part-time job, and sleep becomes a distant memory.

Most counselors recommend: 9th grade (0-1 AP), 10th grade (1-2 APs), 11th grade (2-3 APs), 12th grade (3-4 APs). These are limits, not goals.

Think About College Admissions

Colleges don't just count how many APs you took. They want to see depth. Taking AP English, AP History, and AP Art shows you're a humanities person. Taking AP Physics, AP Calculus, and AP Computer Science shows you're a STEM person.

Taking AP Art, AP Physics, AP French, and AP Economics looks scattered. Choose a lane.

Know the Exam Pass Rates

Some AP exams are notoriously brutal. AP Physics C has a pass rate around 65%. AP Chinese has a pass rate near 85%. This isn't about avoiding hard classes — it's about knowing what you're signing up for.

Talk to Upperclassmen

Ask older students: How much homework? How hard is the teacher? Is the exam fair? Current students will tell you what the course catalog won't.

Don't Take AP Just for the GPA Boost

Many schools give weighted GPAs for AP classes (5.0 instead of 4.0). But a B in AP Physics still lowers your weighted GPA more than an A in regular Physics. Only take AP if you can handle the work.

Your Schedule Is a Zero-Sum Game

Every AP you take replaces something else — an elective you'd enjoy, a study hall you'd need, sleep you'd love. There's no prize for taking the most APs. There's only burnout or balance.

The Golden Rule

One AP you genuinely love and do well in is better than three APs you hate and barely pass. Quality over quantity. Always.

Choose wisely. Your mental health will thank you.
